Matt 5:1-12.literary_note: The Beatitudes open the Sermon on the Mount, which Matthew frames as the New Torah delivered from a mountain (echoing Sinai/Exod 19). The Beatitudes are 8 (or 9 with v.11) macarisms — 'Blessed is/are X who Y' — a form common in OT (Ps 1; 34:8; 119:1-2), Proverbs, and inter-testamental literature. Matthew groups Beatitudes 1-4 under spiritual poverty and need; 5-8 under active virtues/responses. They are descriptive (who the kingdom people are) before they are imperative.

key_terms.note: μακάριοι (blessed/happy): Not a wish ('may they be blessed') but a declaration of present reality. In LXX it translates אַשְׁרֵי (ašrē) — a statement of congratulation or flourishing (Ps 1:1; 32:1). Jesus declares these people are already in a state of blessedness, regardless of circumstances.

πενθοῦντες (those who mourn, v.4): In the OT, mourning is often eschatological — mourning over sin, over exile, over the absence of God's kingdom (Isa 61:1-3). 'Blessed are those who mourn' echoes Isa 61:1 LXX. The comfort is the arrival of the kingdom.

δικαιοσύνη (righteousness, v.6,10): In vv.6 and 10, the same word means different things. V.6: hungering for righteousness (probably God's eschatological vindication/justice); v.10: persecuted for righteousness (ethical uprightness resulting from kingdom loyalty). The semantic range stretches across both.

καθαροὶ τῇ καρδίᾳ (pure in heart, v.8): OT background: Ps 24:4 — 'clean hands and a pure heart' are the qualification for ascending God's holy hill. The promise is the beatific vision: 'they will see God.' Not just in the age to come — the pure in heart perceive God's presence now.

cultural_notes: Macarisms in Second Temple Judaism: The Qumran War Scroll and Psalms of Solomon use similar forms. The Beatitudes signal that Jesus is offering an alternative definition of who is blessed — countering Roman patronage (blessed are the rich, the powerful, the well-connected) with God's inverted economy.

Isaiah 61 background: The Sermon's opening words echo Isa 61:1-3 closely: poor in spirit/poor (61:1), mourning/comfort (61:2), meek/inherit the land (61:7). Jesus is presenting his ministry as the fulfillment of Isaiah's eschatological jubilee.

Sermon setting: Matthew places the Sermon on a mountain; Luke places it 'on a level place' (Luke 6:17-49). Matthew's mountain evokes Moses on Sinai. Jesus gives new Torah with the authority of the Lawgiver himself — hence the antitheses ('You have heard... but I say').
intertextual_connections: Ps 37 — the meek inherit the land (v.11 LXX). Matt 5:5 quotes this verse directly.
Isa 61:1-3 — the Jubilee proclamation. Jesus identifies his audience (the poor, the mourning) with those Isaiah promises to comfort.
Luke 6:20-23 — parallel Beatitudes in the 'Sermon on the Plain': four positive beatitudes and four woes. Matthew's version is more expanded and interiorized ('poor in spirit' vs. simply 'poor').
Matt 5:1-12.synthesis: The Beatitudes are not ethical requirements for earning the kingdom but a portrait of the kingdom people — those who are already inside the eschatological reversal Jesus announces. The consistent logic is: what the world deems unfortunate (poverty of spirit, mourning, meekness, hunger) is precisely what qualifies one for kingdom blessing; and what the world deems admirable (wealth, comfort, power, social honor) is what risks exclusion. This is not pietistic withdrawal — the Beatitudes end with mission: peacemakers, persecuted for righteousness, salt and light (5:13-16). The kingdom community is formed by these reversed values and then sent back into the world to embody them.

tradition_map.patristic: Augustine (De Sermone Domini in Monte) identified 7 Beatitudes with 7 gifts of the Spirit (Isa 11:2-3). Chrysostom read them as a ladder of virtues.
tradition_map.reformation: Calvin: the Beatitudes teach that our salvation is entirely God's grace — those who are 'poor in spirit' (without self-sufficiency) receive the kingdom. Luther: the Beatitudes are descriptive of Christian existence under the cross, not moral programs.
modern_debates: Eschatological vs. ethical: Are the Beatitudes primarily about eschatological reversal (God's future kingdom, inaugurated now) or ethical instruction for present behavior? Most scholars see both dimensions inseparable.

Poverty of spirit vs. poverty: Matthew's 'poor in spirit' vs. Luke's unqualified 'poor.' Is Matthew spiritualizing Luke, or are both describing the same people (the materially poor who know their need before God)?

Matt 26:26-29.pericope_label: The Lord's Supper — This Is My Body
Matt 26:26-29.literary_note: Matthew 26:26-29 records the institution of the Eucharist in the context of the Passover meal (26:17-19). The Passover seder interpreted Exodus redemption through its ritual elements; Jesus now overlays a new interpretive layer: the bread is his body, the cup is his blood. The phrase 'blood of the covenant' (v.28) is a direct citation of Exod 24:8 (Moses at Sinai) and Jer 31:31 (new covenant promise). The eschatological note in v.29 ('I will not drink again... until I drink it new with you in my Father's kingdom') frames the Supper as both memorial and anticipation.

key_terms.note: διαθήκη (covenant): v.28 — 'this is my blood of the covenant.' The word carries the LXX freight of בְּרִית — the solemn binding agreement at the heart of Israel's relationship with God. Jesus is establishing a new covenant (Jer 31:31) through his blood — the sacrifice that seals it.

ἄφεσιν ἁμαρτιῶν (forgiveness of sins): Only Matthew adds this phrase (v.28). Mark and Luke omit it. This interpretive addition makes explicit what is implicit: the blood of the covenant effects the forgiveness that Jer 31:34 promised ('for I will forgive their iniquity').

εὐχαριστήσας (having given thanks, v.27): The verb from which 'Eucharist' derives. Jesus gives thanks over the cup — the standard Jewish blessing (berakah) over wine, now transformed into thanksgiving for his own blood.

cultural_notes: Passover seder context: The third cup of the Passover seder (the 'cup of blessing,' 1 Cor 10:16) is the likely cup Jesus uses. The haggadah commentary of the seder interpreted each element; Jesus provides his own haggadah: the lamb's sacrifice points to him.

Covenant blood: Exod 24:8 — Moses sprinkled blood on the people saying 'behold the blood of the covenant which the LORD has made with you.' Jesus' language deliberately echoes this — his blood is the new covenant ratification.
intertextual_connections: Exod 24:8 — blood of the covenant at Sinai. Jesus' 'blood of the covenant' is a direct citation.
Jer 31:31-34 — new covenant promise: forgiveness of sins, law written on the heart. Matt 26:28 is the inaugural moment of that new covenant.
1 Cor 11:23-26 — Paul's account of the Lord's Supper, the earliest NT record (pre-dating Matthew). The 'do this in remembrance of me' language is Paul's, not Matthew's.
Matt 26:26-29.synthesis: The Last Supper is the moment where Passover becomes fulfilled and surpassed. Jesus does not simply observe the seder — he reinterprets its central elements around himself: the bread is his body (which will be broken), the cup is his blood (which will be shed for forgiveness). The double citation of Exod 24 and Jer 31 is the interpretive key: Jesus is simultaneously the sacrifice that ratifies the new covenant and the fulfillment of what Jeremiah promised — the new covenant that writes the law internally and forgives definitively. The eschatological note (v.29) means the Supper is not only memorial but anticipation: every celebration points forward to the Messianic banquet in the kingdom, when Jesus will drink 'new' with his people. This gives the Eucharist its present meaning: the people of God eat between the ages, remembering the sacrifice and anticipating the consummation.

tradition_map.patristic: Ignatius of Antioch (early 2nd c.) called the Eucharist 'the medicine of immortality.' Justin Martyr described weekly Eucharistic celebration as the distinctively Christian worship.
tradition_map.reformation: Four views crystallized: (1) Transubstantiation (Catholic): bread and wine become body and blood; (2) Consubstantiation (Lutheran): body and blood are truly present 'in, with, and under' the elements; (3) Calvinist/Reformed: real spiritual presence received through faith; (4) Zwinglian/memorial: pure symbol and remembrance.
modern_debates: Real presence vs. memorial: The Greek 'is' (ἐστιν) in v.26 — is this ontological identity or metaphorical predication ('this represents')? The Greek present tense and Aramaic substrate (where Jesus spoke without a copula) both leave the question genuinely open.
Unity and divisions: The Lord's Supper has been the most divisive point among Christians. The Marburg Colloquy (1529) failed when Luther and Zwingli could not agree on v.26.
